Had is used for past perfect sentences.
'Has been' is used when 'since' and 'for' are used in sentences.

questions.explanation: The simple rule is that "is" is present tense and "was" is past tense. If it's happening now, you use "is". If it happened in the past, you use "was".

questions.text: One day people _ to Moon
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Travel is simply used for present tenses.
Traveled is a third form showing past tense.
'Will travel' is the correct option that shows something is coming in the upcoming days.
'Were traveling' shows something continuous in the past.

questions.explanation: 'Will travel' is the correct option that shows something is coming in the upcoming days.

questions.text: Complete the sentences by choosing the most appropriate option:The road is wet. It must _ raining.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: The verb phrase “have been” is the present perfect continuous tense of “to be”. It is used to indicate that an action that started in the indefinite past has come to completion, or is still in progress, at the present. From the given sentence, it can be inferred that “the road is wet” in the present because of ‘rain’ that occurred in the past but evidence of it is seen in the present. Thus, choosing “have been” to complete the given sentence would be most appropriate.

The verb “had” is the past tense and past participle form of the base verb “have”. (Example: It had my favorite toppings.). Additionally, to form the past perfect, use had and the past participle of a verb in one part of the sentence.
The verb “has” is a third person singular subject
The verb “have” is an irregular verb (the verb can be used as a main verb or as an auxiliary verb). As an auxiliary verb, “have” is used to make the present perfect tenses of main verb.

questions.explanation: The tense used here is present perfect tense so the correct helping verb to use here should be "have been".

questions.text: Either my father or my brothers _ going to sell the car.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: ‘Is’ is a singular verb. Hence, this option is incorrect as the sentence required plural verb of help verb.
‘Are’ is a plural verb and is the most suitable option. Hence, this option is correct.
Although ‘were’ is a plural verb, we will not use it because we prefer to use the present tense when possible.
‘Was’ is a singular verb. Hence, it is incorrect.

questions.explanation: If a sentence has two subjects connected by or/nor, either/or, or neither/nor, the verb must agree with the later subject (in this case, ‘brothers’). As the subject is plural, the verb must also be plural.

questions.text: Neither they nor their friend _ solved the question.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This is the correct option. In 'neither...nor' constructions, the verb agrees with the subject closest to it. 'Their friend' is singular, so 'has' is the correct verb.
This is incorrect. Although 'they' is plural and would typically take 'have,' the verb must agree with the subject closest to it, which is 'their friend,' a singular noun.
This is incorrect because 'was' is not suitable for expressing the completion of the action 'solved.' Furthermore, 'was' does not match the present context of solving a question.
This is incorrect. 'Were' is a past tense plural verb which does not match the present context of the sentence. The verb should be singular to match 'friend.'

questions.explanation: In this sentence, 'neither...nor' indicates that the verb should agree with the subject that is closest to it. Since 'friend' is singular, the correct verb is 'has.' The other options are incorrect: 'Have' would be used if the subject closest to the verb were plural; 'Was' and 'Were' do not fit because they do not appropriately express the current action of solving the question.

questions.text: Complete the sentences by choosing the most appropriate option, from the given lettered choices (A to D) below each. The recent discoveries of medical science have_ life and health to millions of people.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: "Brought" is the past participle form of the verb"bring." It is used in the sentence to indicate the action of medicalscience bringing life and health to millions of people. Therefore, "brought" is the correct option as itmatches the tense of the sentence and correctly conveys the action of medicalscience bringing life and health to millions of people.

"Bring" is the base form of the verb, but itdoesn't match the tense of the sentence. The sentence is in the present perfecttense, indicating actions that have occurred in the past with a connection tothe present.

"Had brought" is the past perfect tense, whichimplies an action that happened before another past action. However, there isno need to convey a sequence of past events in this sentence, so the pastperfect tense is not necessary.

"Bringing" is the present participle form of theverb "bring," which indicates an ongoing action. However, thesentence is talking about recent discoveries in the past that have already hadan impact on millions of people, so the present participle form is notsuitable.

questions.explanation: The question is talking about a thing that has already happened, so bringing and bring would not be correct options. Option C changes the word to “bought” which has a totally different meaning so is incorrect. So option A is correct.

questions.text: The headmaster _ to speak to you.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: “The headmaster wants to speak to you” in Option A sounds most appropriate because "headmaster" is a singular noun so we should complement it with a singular verb - eliminating Option D. The tense is simple present indicated by the verb "speak", hence the present continuous form "is wanting" in Option B is also incorrect. while the past tense form in Option C is also incorrect.

“The headmaster wants to speak to you” in Option A sounds most appropriate because "headmaster" is a singular noun so we should complement it with a singular verb - eliminating Option D. The tense is simple present indicated by the verb "speak", hence the present continuous form "is wanting" in Option B is also incorrect. while the past tense form in Option C is also incorrect.

“The headmaster wants to speak to you” in Option A sounds most appropriate because "headmaster" is a singular noun so we should complement it with a singular verb - eliminating Option D. The tense is simple present indicated by the verb "speak", hence the present continuous form "is wanting" in Option B is also incorrect. while the past tense form in Option C is also incorrect.

“The headmaster wants to speak to you” in Option A sounds most appropriate because "headmaster" is a singular noun so we should complement it with a singular verb - eliminating Option D. The tense is simple present indicated by the verb "speak", hence the present continuous form "is wanting" in Option B is also incorrect. while the past tense form in Option C is also incorrect.

questions.explanation: “The headmaster wants to speak to you” in Option A sounds most appropriate because "headmaster" is a singular noun so we should complement it with a singular verb - eliminating Option D. The tense is simple present indicated by the verb "speak", hence the present continuous form "is wanting" in Option B is also incorrect. while the past tense form in Option C is also incorrect.

questions.text: Choose the correct option: Knowledge and wisdom _ no time for connection.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because "has" is the singular form of the verb "to have," used with singular subjects like "knowledge" or "wisdom." However, in this sentence, "knowledge and wisdom" are treated as a plural compound subject, so a plural verb form is required.

This is the correct answer. "Have" is the plural form of the verb "to have," used with plural subjects like "knowledge and wisdom." In this sentence, "knowledge and wisdom" are considered together as a plural entity requiring a plural verb.
This option is incorrect because "had" is the past tense form of "have." It does not fit the present tense context of the sentence.
This option is incorrect because "are" is the present tense form of the verb "to be," not "have." It does not match with the verb needed to complete the sentence correctly.

questions.explanation: The context of the sentence firstly requires a verb that satisfies the need to indicate the possession of "no time for connection" by "Knowledge and wisdom." Therefore the verb forms "is/am/are" which indicate "being" are not suitable here - eliminating Option D. Moreover, since the need for "no time" is being stated as a universal statement (everlasting) i.e. started in the past and continues to be true in the present, the tense should be present perfect. Hence, the verb form should include the helping verb "Have" as indicated by Option B.

questions.text: Ahmed _ me for a long time.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because "know" is the base form (infinitive) of the verb and does not fit the tense needed in the sentence.

This is the correct answer. "Has known" is the present perfect tense of the verb "know." It indicates that Ahmed started knowing (or became acquainted with) the speaker in the past and this acquaintance has continued up to the present moment.
This option is incorrect because "knows" is the present tense form of the verb, which does not fit the context of the sentence describing a past duration.

This option is incorrect because "knew" is the past tense form of the verb "know." It describes a completed action in the past, which does not indicate a continuing state from the past to the present.

questions.explanation: The most appropriate word to fill in the blank would be "has known."The complete sentence would be: "Ahmed has known me for a long time."Using the present perfect tense "has known" indicates that Ahmed developed knowledge or familiarity with the speaker in the past and that this knowledge or familiarity has continued up until the present time.

questions.text: Not only the parents but also their son _ for interview.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because "has called" implies that the son is the one who made phone calls. The sentence structure, however, suggests that the son is the one who received a call or invitation.
This option is incorrect because "have called" is plural, but the subject "son" is singular. The verb form should agree with the singular subject.
This option is incorrect because "have been called" is plural, but the subject "son" is singular.

This is the correct answer. "Has been called" is in the passive voice and indicates that the son received a call or invitation for an interview. This fits the sentence structure and meaning intended, as it focuses on the son being invited or contacted.

questions.explanation: Parents are plural but the blank follows the noun 'son' which is singular. Hence "has" instead of "have" is the right option.The sentence refers to an act being done to the noun instead of the son doing something. Hence it will be: ‘been called’ instead of just ‘called’

questions.text: Complete the sentence by choosing the most appropriate option:There _ many students waiting to hear the results of the test.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Students are plural so the helping verb should also be plural, which are "have" and "are". So that eliminates option A and C. As the verb in the sentence is in continuous form so the correct option is D "are".
Option B is incorrect because Have cannot be used here as it is used in the sentence having 3rd form of verb i.e. Perfect tense.

Students are plural so the helping verb should also be plural, which are "have" and "are". So that eliminates option A and C. Moreover, "was" is used with singular nouns. As the verb in the sentence is in continuous form so the correct option is D "are".

Students are plural. So, the helping verb should also be plural, which is "have" and "are". So that eliminates option A and C. As the verb in the sentence is in continuous form so the correct option is D i.e "are".

questions.explanation: Students are plural. So, the helping verb should also be plural, which are “have” and “are”.Have can not be used here because it is used in perfect tense which has the 3rd form of verb while here we have the continuous form of verb i.e. “waiting”. So, the answer is D.

questions.text: Select the best word or words to complete the sentence.In her free time, Veronica loves _, reading, and trying new recipes.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This is the base form of the verb, and it doesn't match the "-ing" form of the other activities in the list. It would break the parallel structure and sound awkward in the sentence.
This is the correct choice. It is the present participle form (-ing form) of the verb "dance." By using "dancing," the sentence maintains a parallel structure with the other activities.
The infinitive form "to dance" would also break the parallel structure because it doesn't match the "-ing" form of the other activities in the list.
This is an incorrect combination because it uses the infinitive form "to" with the present participle form "-ing" of the verb. It is grammatically incorrect and doesn't fit the sentence structure.

questions.explanation: The best word to complete the sentence is B) dancing. This is because the sentence uses a list of gerunds (verbs ending in -ing that act as nouns) and the items in the list should be parallel in form. Therefore, dance should also be a gerund.

questions.text: Fill in the blank with the appropriate option:Lions, like any other carnivores, _ on meat.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: In this sentence, the subject “lions” is plural, referring to more than one lion. Therefore, we need to use the plural verb form “live” to agree with the subject.
“Lives” is incorrect because it uses the singular verb form which does not agree with the plural subject.
“Does live” is incorrect because it uses the singular verb “does” along with the base form of the verb “live”. This structure is not necessary or appropriate in this context.
“Living” is incorrect because it uses the present participle form of the verb “live”. In this sentence, we need a finite verb form to match the subject “lions”.

questions.explanation: Since the noun is plural, hence we will use a singular verb: live.

questions.text: Fill in the blank:I can’t walk _
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This is incorrect in this sentence because “farther” is typically used when referring to a physical distance or literal movement.

This is incorrect because it's an adverb that generally refers to distance, but does not convey the intended meaning of the sentence which is the speaker’s limitation in their ability to continue walking.
According to the sentence, the person means that he cannot walk anymore, so FURTHER, WHICH MEANS ADDITIONAL is the correct answer.
This option does not fit the context of the sentence because it suggests a movement in a direction away from a particular location.

questions.explanation: Further is used to refer to 'more or additional.' whereas, Farther is used to mention a 'greater distance.' The sentence given in the question means that I cannot walk anymore. Hence, "Further" will be the correct option.

questions.text: Complete the sentences by choosing the most appropriate option, from the given lettered choices (A to D) below each:Birds usually _ eggs in their nests.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: 'Laid' is the past tense of 'lay', which is not suitable for expressing a habitual action.
'Lain' is the past participle of 'lie', which means to recline, and is not applicable here.
'Lay' is the present tense form of the verb meaning to place something down, suitable for describing a habitual action of birds.
'Lie' means to recline or not tell the truth, which does not fit the context of the sentence.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 'lay'. The sentence describes a habitual action, indicated by the word 'usually', which requires the present tense form of the verb. 'Lay' is the correct verb form for describing the action of birds placing eggs in their nests. 'Laid' is past tense, 'lain' is a past participle of 'lie' meaning to recline, and 'lie' can mean either recline or not tell the truth, neither of which fit the context of the sentence.

questions.text: Select the option which fits appropriately in the blank:The gardner will have _ the flowers.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: PLUCKED (Correct)"Will have + past participle" is the future perfect tense, used to describe an action that will be completed in the future before a specific time or event."Plucked" is the past participle of "pluck"."The gardener will have plucked the flowers." (Correct)
PLUCK"Pluck" is the base form (present tense), but here we need past participle for future perfect tense, so wrong."The gardener will have pluck the flowers." (Incorrect)
PLUCKS "Plucks" is present simple tense (he/she/it plucks), but it doesn't fit here because we need past participle for future perfect."The gardener will have plucks the flowers." (Incorrect)

options.text: None of these options are correct
options.explanation: NONE of these options are correct.We cannot leave this blank because the verb is needed to complete the future perfect tense structure.

questions.explanation: "Plucked" is the correct verb form.The future perfect tense refers to a completed activity in the future.

questions.text: Select the option which fits appropriately in the blank:The robber broke into the house and _ (tie) the lone occupant with ropes.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This is the correct past tense form of the verb "tie." It matches the sequence of events described in the sentence, where the action of tying the lone occupant with ropes occurred after the robber broke into the house.
This option is incorrect because "ties" is the present tense form of the verb "tie," which does not fit the past tense context of the sentence.
This option is incorrect because "tie" is the base form (infinitive) of the verb and does not form a complete verb phrase in the context of the sentence.

This option is incorrect because "tying" is the present participle (-ing form) of the verb "tie," used to indicate an ongoing action or a continuous tense. It does not fit the past tense context required by the sentence.

questions.explanation: This is the correct past tense form of the verb "tie." It matches the sequence of events described in the sentence, where the action of tying the lone occupant with ropes occurred after the robber broke into the house.
